denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Weekly psychotherapy beyond 20 visits per plan year related to Generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1).

After clinical review using Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletins (CPBs) and InterQual criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Annual behavioral health visit cap of 20 has been reached. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Visit cadence requested exceeds insurer's guideline (e.g., PT > 20 visits).

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Weekly psychotherapy beyond 20 visits per plan year. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management
clinical_context:
The 49-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Weekly psychotherapy beyond 20 visits per plan year as the next appropriate step.

MHPAEA parity: medical specialty visits are not capped at 20. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on frequency excessive without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
